dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 for reported hemorrhage/lacerations at Apt # 406, Richmond.
Hello, my name is Jessie, a paramedic with the squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I have been feeling weak and sick for a couple of days now. I've had nausea, vomiting, and blood in my stool.
You're fully alert and oriented. You are A&Ox4 with a GCS of 15.
You mentioned nausea and vomiting. Did you experience any dizziness with these symptoms?
No, I haven't experienced dizziness, but I do have chills and a low-grade fever at times.
Blood pressure is 97/63.
Respirations are 26 per minute.
SpO₂ is at 98%.
Pulse is 127 and strong at the radial.
Blood glucose is 158.
Temperature is 37.4 in the ear.
He started feeling unwell two days ago after breakfast-then it really kicked in after lunch.
EMS did not observe altered mental status. All signs consistent with baseline.
Have you experienced or noticed any chest pain or shortness of breath?
No chest pain reported or observed. No shortness of breath either.
Any cough or production noted recently?
No signs of cough.
May I take a moment to collect some personal information for our records?
Your medical history includes lung and kidney cancer along with recent hospitalizations. Do you have osteoarthritis or any other medical conditions?
Yes, I also have osteoarthritis in my knees.
Are there any allergies to medications or food we should be aware of?
I am allergic to aspirin.
Are you currently experiencing any pain or discomfort?
No, I'm experiencing zero pain at the moment.
Based on the call type reported at dispatch, we were alerted to potential external bleeding or lacerations. Have you noticed any cuts or wounds?
No, I wasn't bleeding at any point. My main issue is the nausea and blood in my stool.
Your skin appears pink, warm, and dry with a strong radial pulse and no external bleeding or edema noted.
12-lead ECG reveals a right bundle branch block and sinus tachycardia. No ST elevations, dysrhythmias, or ectopy detected.
Chest movement is equal and symmetric with no deformity or signs of pain.
Breathing appears adequate, with no signs of distress.
Have you experienced any cough or sputum production?
No signs of cough or sputum.
Blood pressure is now 109/72.
Respirations are now 30 per minute.
SpO₂ remains at 100% on ambient air.
Current pulse is 140 and consistent on the monitor.
May I place the 4-lead monitor on you for continuous assessment?
Yes, go ahead. Do what you must.
4-lead placed and monitor is active.
Would you allow me to place a 20-gauge IV on your right arm?
I'll start fluids and administer medications following our nausea/vomiting protocol.
Vitals now read 109/72 with a pulse of 109 and respiratory rate of 26.
500mL of normal saline has been administered through the IV.
Zofran, 4mg IVP, has been administered for nausea and vomiting.
Vitals are now 130/87 with a pulse of 109 and respirations at 26.
With tachycardia, respiratory changes, fever, and low ETCO2 noted, a sepsis alert has been called to VCU.
You will be transported to VCU Health for further treatment and care.
Let me assist you onto the stretcher. Please lie back and remain still when I help.
Stretcher is secured. Harness in place for safe transport.
Have you consented to transport and treatment?
Yes, please move quickly; I'm not feeling well at the moment.
Consent for transport and billing has been signed on record.
Ambulance en route. We're monitoring you continuously for any changes in condition.
Blood pressure is steady at 116/83 with all other vital signs stable.
We've arrived at VCU. Pre-hospital report will be given at the ER upon transfer.
Final vitals are pulse 95 with no further changes in any assessed parameters.
No external signs of bleeding.
This will be a cold transfer to the ER. The hospital is aware and prepared for your arrival.
All procedures accounted for during transport, and all protocols followed as outlined in the medical directive.
Direct hand-off to ER staff will begin shortly. My partner and I will provide a verbal report now.
